name: Tengu
description: Tengu are legendary creatures found in Japanese folk religion and are also considered a type of yōkai (supernatural beings). They were originally thought to be disruptive demons and harbingers of war, but their image gradually softened over time, eventually becoming associated with the ascetic practices of Shugendō. They are often depicted as protectors of the mountains and forests, and are known for their martial prowess and mastery of magic. There are two main types of Tengu: Kotengu (lesser Tengu) and Daitengu (greater Tengu). Daitengu are generally depicted with long noses, while Kotengu often have bird-like beaks. They are often associated with vanity and pride, and are known to punish those who are arrogant or disrespectful. Despite their sometimes mischievous or malevolent nature, they can also be benevolent and offer guidance to those who are worthy. Tengu are skilled swordsmen and are said to have taught the art of swordsmanship to legendary warriors. They possess great strength, can fly, and wield magical powers, including illusion and shape-shifting. They are fierce protectors of their domain and will fiercely defend it against intruders.
subtitle: Mountain dwelling tricksters and protectors
appearance: Tengu vary in appearance. Daitengu are typically depicted as humanoid with long noses, red faces, and sometimes white hair. They often wear yamabushi (mountain ascetic) clothing, including small black caps and robes. Kotengu often have bird-like features, such as beaks and wings, but can also appear more human. Both types are often depicted carrying fans or staffs and are associated with the mountains.
habitat: Tengu primarily reside in mountainous regions, particularly in deep forests and remote areas. They are considered guardians of the mountains and are often associated with sacred sites and temples located in these areas.
behavior: Tengu are often depicted as proud, solitary, and easily offended creatures. They are known for their martial skills and are quick to punish those who disrespect them or the mountains they protect. While they can be mischievous and even malevolent, they can also be benevolent and offer guidance to those who are respectful and humble. They are often depicted as teachers of martial arts and magic.

keyMyths.title: Minamoto no Yoshitsune and the Tengu
keyMyths.description: The legendary warrior Minamoto no Yoshitsune is said to have been trained in swordsmanship by a Tengu in his youth. This training helped him become one of the most skilled warriors in Japanese history, showcasing the Tengu's martial prowess.

keyMyths.title: The Arrogant Priest and the Tengu
keyMyths.description: A story tells of an arrogant priest who mocked the Tengu's appearance. The Tengu punished him by leading him astray in the mountains, highlighting the Tengu's aversion to pride and disrespect.

sources.author: Michael Dylan Foster
sources.work: The Book of Yokai: Mysterious Creatures of Japanese Folklore
sources.description: A comprehensive guide to various Yokai, including detailed information on the Tengu's history and variations.
sources.text: A comprehensive guide to various Yokai, including detailed information on the Tengu's history and variations.

sources.author: Carmen Blacker
sources.work: The Catalpa Bow: A Study of Shamanistic Practices in Japan
sources.description: Explores the connection between Tengu and Shugendo practices, providing insight into their spiritual significance.
